does: This paper presents an autonomous multiagent framework that automates mechanistic interpretability of large language models through two coupled processes. The first loop generates competing hypotheses about what internal features do, tests them with targeted prompts and prompt controls, and evaluates explanations across multiple metrics to iteratively refine interpretations. The second loop discovers novel features by generating diverse prompt sets, constructing k-nearest-neighbor graphs in activation space, and identifying candidate features based on statistical separability and semantic coherence. The authors demonstrate improvements over one-shot autointerpreteation baselines on Gemma-2 models and weight-sparse transformers, discovering interpretable language-specific and safety-relevant features while producing auditable explanation traces that are more falsifiable than static labels.
connects: This work builds on the growing mechanistic interpretability literature that seeks to understand learned computations in neural networks, extending recent approaches like automated neuron explanation and feature attribution beyond single-pass methods. It combines insights from feature activation research, which uses activation patterns to identify neurons responding to specific concepts, with adversarial testing methodologies that validate model behavior through targeted probes. The use of k-nearest-neighbor graphs in activation space extends clustering-based approaches to feature discovery, while the hypothesis-testing framework draws from causal inference and scientific methodology applied to neural networks. This directly responds to limitations in one-shot auto-interpretation methods by introducing iterative refinement loops, building on prior work on agent-based reasoning and prompt engineering for model analysis.
enables: This framework opens several future directions for scalable mechanistic interpretability. The automated explanation and discovery pipeline could extend to larger models and broader feature classes, potentially supporting systematic safety audits of language model internals before deployment. The auditable trace generation provides a foundation for more transparent model debugging and feature documentation at scale. The multiagent approach suggests paths toward fully automated model understanding that could identify unexpected or emergent features without human-specified hypotheses. This work also enables better feature attribution for model editing and intervention, as well as more rigorous evaluation of whether interpreted features actually control model behavior, supporting the broader goal of interpretable and controllable AI systems.
